text:
= 1 =


December 2021

Hearing Transcript

LAMAR: Will you require the Bible?

HUNT: No, thank you.

LAMAR: Please raise your right hand. Do you swear to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth?

HUNT: I do.

LAMAR: I understand that you have information relevant to this commission?

HUNT: That's correct.

LAMAR: Please give your testimony.

HUNT: Where should I start? The day of the accident?

LAMAR: No. At the beginning.

June 2021

Nate Hunt was waiting for his wife. Lucy was late.

"Dad, I'm bored," moaned Eliza, their daughter. Nate had her on his lap. The girl's hair had gone staticky from rubbing against his sweater; it tickled his nose.

"Yeah, I know," he sighed. "You already said that."

Nate was bored too. All the ceremonies, tours, and press conferences seemed as though they would never end. At the same time he was terrified. It might have seemed that those two emotional states were mutually exclusive, but they weren't, not at all. He had been through this once before, eight years earlier. Only back then he'd been here alone. He hadn't had to rehearse in his head what he would say to a child if something went wrong. He still couldn't find the right words. Maybe there simply weren't any.

"When's Mom coming?" the girl asked.

"Soon," he said.

"But exactly how soon?"

"I don't know, sweetheart."

"Ty khochesh konfetu, rebyonka?" The woman in the next chair rustled a bag of hard candies and smiled encouragingly. But Eliza was in no mood to talk to strangers. She turned away from the woman and burrowed into her father.

"Prostite, ona sonnaya." Nate smiled apologetically. He had learned Russian more or less by accident. He had quizzed Lucy before her exams so many times that bits and pieces had stuck. His favorite word, pochemuchka, someone who asks too many questions, suited Eliza to a tee.

The woman nodded in understanding; she had probably been up since before dawn too. Which time was this for her, Nate wondered. The third? The fourth? She didn't look nervous; on the contrary, the broad smile never left her lips. Maybe because along with the candy she also had a pack of tranquilizers in her purse.

"Let's at least play a game," Eliza said.

"Sure," Nate said. "How about I Spy?"

"Okay. You first."

Nate looked around the room. It resembled a classroom or a dentist's waiting room: harsh fluorescent light, beige walls decorated with faded posters, rows of plastic chairs, stunted plants on the windowsill. Except that a thick plexiglass divider ran through the middle of the room. On the other side stood a long table, and on it three microphones, each labeled, Cyrillic on top, the Latin alphabet underneath. Anton Kovalov. Lev Zaitsev. Lucy Poplaski.

"I spy something beginning with M," Nate whispered.

"Map?" Eliza asked.

"No."

"Magazine rack?"

He shook his head.

"Microphone?"

"Yes. Your turn."

The girl began to fidget, paying no attention at all to the fact that she kept digging an elbow or a knee into her father's stomach. The people gathered in the room - members of the other families, officials, reporters - waved at her and smiled. But Eliza ignored them completely; she was focused on the game. She took any kind of competition very seriously. Once, when she came second in a sack race at a school event, she cried for the rest of the day. "Eliza is a very ambitious girl," her teacher told them in parting, in a tone that suggested she might even be too ambitious. "She gets that from her mother," Nate replied without a moment's hesitation.

"I spy something beginning with R," she said.

"A row of chairs?"

"No."

"Hmm..." Nate rubbed his chin. "A room divider?"

"No."

"A railing?"

"Not that either." The girl shook her head. With evident satisfaction.

"Then I give up."

"Rocket!" Eliza cried in triumph.

Nate looked out the window. Amid an endless expanse of sun-scorched grass, it rose toward the sky - surrounded by a halo of milky-white vapor, peering through the latticework of the scaffolding. It did not belong in that landscape: the faded steppes of Kazakhstan, the crumbling, peeling buildings, the howling of stray dogs. It looked like a remnant of some extinct alien civilization. In a sense, that was exactly what it was: the Baikonur Cosmodrome had, after all, been built by the Soviet Union. There were two reasons for putting it here. First, the latitude made it easier to reach the desired orbit. Second, if something went wrong - if the rocket exploded just after liftoff, for instance - the risk of burning scraps of metal falling on someone's head was relatively small. As far as the eye could see, there was nothing but grass.

Nate had seen that kind of explosion before. He was six at the time, visiting family in Florida, and it so happened that the much-postponed launch of the shuttle Challenger was drawing near. His parents took him to Kennedy Space Center so he could see it with his own eyes, especially since Christa McAuliffe was on the crew, an ordinary schoolteacher who was going to teach a lesson from space. He remembered it with a child's memory, as if he'd had a camera with him then and kept taking random pictures: badly framed, out of focus, overexposed with emotion. He got a paper cap with a NASA logo whose brim kept slipping down over his eyes, as a result of which he was very sad. They found seats in the stands directly behind a man in a cowboy hat. He blocked the whole view, so little Nate was by then in a sulk with the whole world. His father hoisted him onto his shoulders so he could see better, ignoring the complaints from the row behind them. Then came the blinding flash and the roar, the hot air in his hair, the vibrations spreading through his whole body until his teeth rang, until his skin prickled. The rocket climbed, drowning out the cheers, puking fire, faster and faster, until it turned into a tiny dot in the sky. Then that dot flared and became several smaller dots. Some of them started to fall, while others traced fanciful loops in the air. For a fraction of a second Nate thought that was exactly how it was supposed to be, that these were space fireworks, like on the Fourth of July, and he was so happy. The joy did not last long, and what came afterward -- sobs, screams, chaos -- kept coming back to him in dreams for years. Even so, he, too, bound up his life with space. The vastness fascinated, terrified, drew him in.

A murmur passed through the room, camera shutters rattling one after another. At last, Nate exhaled. On the other side of the glass partition, the door opened and the soyuz crew came in -- Lev, Anton, and Lucy. All the lenses were trained on his wife. First because she was, without question, more photogenic than the Russians. Dark curly hair tied back in a ponytail, a broad smile, an upturned freckled nose -- she looked like a friendly girl next door, someone you could befriend in five minutes flat. Second, she was one of the last American women who would fly into space from Kazakhstan. After more than a dozen years, the United States finally had its own transport to orbit again: Dragon capsules, built -- in the best American tradition -- by an eccentric billionaire. The first flights had been successful, and NASA was already negotiating a new, larger contract that would end American dependence on Russian assistance altogether. The clock was ticking.

"Uvazhaemye kollegi, dorogie gosti..." began Roscosmos director Yuri Rybkin, a gray-haired, broad-shouldered man in a cream blazer.

"What's that man saying?" asked Eliza.

"Nothing interesting."

"But what?"

"That a beautiful chapter in the history of friendship between our nations is coming to an end, but a new one is beginning."

"What does that mean?"

That soon the Russians would no longer be able to collect ninety million dollars for a seat in a Soyuz, Nate thought. That without those regular cash injections Roscosmos would be on the brink of bankruptcy. That behind the declarations of readiness for further cooperation lurked envy, reproach, and resentment.

"That we'll keep flying into space together, but apart," he said instead.

The Russian official finished his speech, and the microphone passed to NASA's representative, Steve Ayers, the deputy director for crewed spaceflight - a stocky, balding man with thick glasses. More lofty platitudes, wishes for a successful mission, agency newspeak. At last it was time to say goodbye to family: forced intimacy in front of cameras, among strangers, through plexiglass. Nate sat down with his daughter opposite Lucy. He wanted to tell her so many things. That he was so damn proud. That he'd miss her. That he was glad. That he was afraid for her. But he couldn't make himself do it, not in that crowd.

"Hey." She spoke first. "How's your morning been?"

So, banalities. Empty words. But empty words can be filled with something, after all; they can ring with something entirely unrelated to what they actually mean. Tone of voice, a pause, a glance: that was how they told each other everything that couldn't be said, everything they wouldn't even have known how to name, in defiance of semantics.

"Fine," Nate said. "Right, Eliza? Fine?"

"When are you coming back?" the girl asked. The question was an accusation too.

"You know that. Six months."

"You won't be here for my birthday."

"I will, sweetheart," Lucy said with a sad smile. "More than once, in fact."

"What?"

"Remember? The station orbits the Earth once every ninety minutes. If your party lasts three hours, I'll pass over the house twice in that time. And I'll be thinking about you then. Okay?"

"It's not the same."

Lucy pressed her palm to the glass. A substitute for real contact, a quarantine requirement.

"I know, sweetheart."

"Then why are you going up there?"

Lucy didn't have time to answer. The time for farewells was over. The next time they saw each other would be in six months. Assuming everything went well.

The Orthodox priest's robe, embroidered with gold thread, glittered in the camera flashes. The sprinkler traced a broad arc through the air, and holy water ran down Lucy's cheeks like tears. Another tradition, another superstition. Every stage of preparation for the flight -- and there were many -- was bound up, for the Russians, with some ritual, and after every mission another one was added; more folklore than at a village wedding. Everyone on the flight must lay flowers at Yuri Gagarin's monument. Run their country's flag up the pole. Plant a sapling in the Cosmonauts' Alley. Sign the hotel door in Baikonur. On the eve of launch, watch White Sun of the Desert, a Soviet hit from the seventies. On top of that, there was, of course, the mandatory blessing from a clergyman, regardless of personal preference or faith, not once but twice. As if the Russians believed the flight's success depended not on the laws of nature or mathematical equations, but on the will of fickle heavens, which had to be appeased with ceremonies that went on for weeks.

Lucy boarded the bus that would take the crew to the launch pad. She was already in her Sokol suit: custom-tailored, fitted with dozens of laces, buttons, and snaps, a veritable space corset. Getting into it took nearly two hours and required the help of several technicians, and because it was fully airtight, each crew member had also been issued a portable fan -- a little blue suitcase -- so they wouldn't get cooked alive.

When the bus set off, when at last Lucy was out of range of cameras and microphones, if only for a moment, she leaned her forehead against the window and closed her eyes. She'd been up since four in the morning, it was nearing six in the evening, and she still had a three-hour flight to the International Space Station ahead of her in a capsule the size of an elevator -- so she had to grab every chance to rest; besides, the Russians were already asleep.

She, however, couldn't sleep. Her thoughts kept returning to Eliza's question. Why? What was the point? Of course, she had answered it thousands of times already: first during the endless selection interviews, then in interviews, public appearances, and in private. But then she said what was expected of her, what she was supposed to say, what other astronauts had been repeating for decades: that this was how she would serve science, that space exploration was humanity's most important mission, that she wanted to inspire little girls all over the world, and so on and so forth. Except those were lies. If someone gave her truth serum -- if such a thing existed -- her answer would be less lofty. Why? Because nothing matches the kick, the sheer fun, of flying at thirteen thousand kilometers an hour. Because it's an incredible adventure. Because others couldn't, and I could. Because it's confirmation that I'm the best.

All this only made the resentment in her daughter's voice, her reproach, hurt more. It wasn't really about the birthday. Lucy was almost never home. She had spent the last four years living out of a suitcase, flying between Washington, Houston, Toulouse, Moscow, and Cologne—endless training sessions, courses, preparations, exams. Every hour—every minute, really—of the mission had to be mapped out and rehearsed over and over at least a year in advance. Lucy had to know every safety procedure by heart, have all the instructions and technical diagrams down cold, know the name of every screw, switch, and wire, in English and in Russian. If she had truly been doing all this for the good of humanity, maybe she could have justified it to herself, quieted her guilty conscience. But that wasn't true. She wondered whether her colleagues had thoughts like these too, whether they too mourned the first steps and first words they had missed, or whether this was simply women's lot: to feel guilty for their own ambition.

Something else was gnawing at her as well. The day before, she had met with Deputy Director Ayers. It had not been scheduled, it wasn't on any calendar, and the words spoken there would be nowhere to be found in any press accounts. Rising tensions. Conflict at the highest levels. Fear of escalation. It was hard to believe that something like this could happen in the twenty-first century, and in blood-soaked Europe at that. But Ayers had been entirely serious. Clouds were gathering on the horizon.

The International Space Station was a child of nineties optimism. Freed from the logic of the Cold War, Russians and Americans no longer had to race one another among the stars, so they joined forces for the good of humanity. Together, with Europe and Japan participating as well, they built a laboratory orbiting the Earth—for the modest sum of a hundred and twenty billion dollars. A symbol of a new age, of shared dreams, of the end of history. Of course, behind the lofty slogans lay pragmatic calculations. The Americans coveted access to Soviet technology, while the Russians needed money to keep their space program alive amid the chaos of perestroika, and at the time no one but the Americans could put up the sums required. Everyone got what they wanted, and everyone was satisfied.

But now, more than twenty years later, the sums no longer added up. First, technically speaking, Roscosmos no longer had anything to offer the Americans: it still relied on Soviet solutions from the 1960s. Second, a new superpower had emerged, China, ready to pour money into the Russian agency—and without tiresome questions about human rights, democracy, or freedom of the press. In other words, the marriage of convenience had begun to crumble, but the house in space still had to be shared.

The bus stopped out in the open, a few kilometers from the rocket; the chirping of crickets carried across the steppe. Right. Time for the last tradition. She got up, and Anton let her out into the aisle with an exaggeratedly gallant gesture. Broad-shouldered, square-jawed, with short blond hair, he was even handsome. Only his teeth, yellowed by cigarettes, spoiled the effect. They had served together on the ISS when she flew there for the first time, eight years earlier. They knew each other well. Which was exactly why she avoided him.

"Ladies to the left, gentlemen to the right," Anton ordered when they stepped onto the hot asphalt. A step behind him came Lev: a head shorter, reserved, his mustache every bit as bushy as his eyebrows. Lucy had met him during joint training sessions and liked him. He was old-school, sincere, devoted to the cause. He deeply believed that the gulf between East and West could be bridged, or at least that from orbit the division stopped making sense. He was the embodiment of the Soviet ideal: hardworking, rational, convinced that the future could be better than the past. This was his last mission, his farewell to space. He looked as if it saddened him already.

A moment later buckles clacked open, zippers hissed. The Sokol suits, which barely an hour earlier had been carefully zipped and fastened by a swarm of technicians in latex gloves, cotton caps, and plastic shoe covers, before the eyes of families and journalists, were now unsealed. Of course everyone, those very technicians included, knew this was exactly what would happen. Because on April 12, 1961, in exactly the same place, Yuri Gagarin had asked the driver to stop the bus. The man who at any moment was to be the first to cast off the shackles of gravity first had to deal with the most earthbound of needs: he had to pee. And since he then flew and came back in one piece, every cosmonaut after him made the same stop, just in case. As usual, the men had it easier. Women, to satisfy the demands of tradition, brought along a plastic bottle of urine collected beforehand. Lucy poured its contents over the rear tire, then shook off the last drops with a firm flick of the wrist she'd picked up from the priest who had blessed them earlier.

The official cosmonauts' anthem, Trava u doma, blared from the loudspeakers, signaling that the crew were already aboard the rocket and less than two hours remained until launch. Steve Ayers wondered how many times he had already heard that plaintive tune, how many times he had stood on the observation deck at the Baikonur Cosmodrome. The twentieth? The twenty-fifth? He had no idea. He had seen off so many missions here that they blurred together; his passport was full of stamps from Kazakh customs. But this moment, this last stretch, still got the adrenaline going. In a moment they would send people into space. There was no getting used to that; it never stopped being astonishing. A few more photographs, a few speeches, a teleconference with mission control in Moscow, and the officials stepped out onto the observation deck. It was nearing eight in the evening.

"One thing ends, another begins." Director Rybkin leaned against the railing. A leaflet with the day's program, folded into quarters, stuck out of his back pocket. Rybkin had come up through the military, and you could tell he hadn't spent it behind a desk. His eyes gave him away: alert, focused, always moving, trained to watch for enemies and seek out traps. The habit might still prove useful. His predecessor had been dismissed less than a year earlier amid scandal, accused of corruption that could earn him a long stay in the gulag. No one wondered whether he was guilty or not; one glance at the jewelry worn by his much younger wife was enough to form an opinion. More puzzling was why the prosecutor's office had turned its attention to him precisely now. In other words: how had he disappointed his patrons? What expectations had he failed to meet? What had changed in the meantime behind the Kremlin's thick walls?

"It doesn't have to end," Ayers replied. They were speaking English; it was easier that way.

"Ah, so you do want to buy a seat on another flight after all?"

"If you can undercut the competition on price..."

"Right... the free market." Rybkin grimaced. "Unfortunately, I don't think that's possible."

"There are other projects."

"Such as?"

Ayers lifted his gaze pointedly skyward. The sun had already set, and the Moon showed through the clouds. The last time a human being had stood on its surface was more than fifty years ago. There had been no shortage of plans to return, but what was lacking was enthusiasm, money -- and a good reason. Over the course of his long career at NASA, Ayers had repeatedly watched projects for a return to the Silver Globe go into the shredder. Just as he was beginning to lose hope that anything would come of it, that within his lifetime people would once again fly beyond Earth orbit, the situation changed completely. SpaceX -- a private company, a start-up that industry veterans had mocked only a dozen or so years earlier -- had shown that you could fly more cheaply, more often, and farther than anyone had dared dream. What was more, advances in nuclear fusion meant that the long-standing dream of using the vast reserves of lunar helium-3 as a revolutionary new fuel had a chance of becoming reality. America was looking up at the night sky with growing interest. And not just America.

"We'll find another partner." Rybkin stepped away from the railing and clasped his hands behind his back.

"China?"

"Do you have something against that?"

"China won't be your partner."

"And you were?"



Ayers did not answer at once. He felt that from here on, he should weigh every word.

"I'd like to think so," he said.

The Russian turned his gaze toward the rocket. Bathed in floodlights, amid the black night of the steppe, it looked like a colossal monument erected in honor of -- what? Human curiosity? Power? Arrogance?

"Do you know how much we pay Kazakhstan to use Baikonur?" Rybkin asked after a moment.

"The figures are publicly available."

"Say it out loud. Let's remind ourselves."

"One hundred and fifteen million a year."

"But one hundred and fifteen million of what? Rubles, perhaps?"

"No. Dollars."

"Dollars, exactly." The Russian nodded. "Is that a lot of money or a little?"

"To me? A lot."

"Right? And how much money did Kazakhstan put into building this cosmodrome that it now graciously lets us use? Are those figures publicly available too, Mr. Ayers?"

"You're trying to drag me into a conversation about politics. And I..."

"Please, just tell me: how much?"

Ayers looked Rybkin in the eye. He was trying to understand where the man was going with this, what he wanted to achieve. Whether this was only about old grievances, or something more.

"At the time, Kazakhstan was part of the Soviet Union," the American replied diplomatically.

"Ah, right. And what, exactly, happened to make the Soviet Union fall apart?"

"I don't think that's relevant to our conversation."

"It's relevant to every conversation."



"Let's say there are different opinions."

"Yes, very different," Rybkin agreed. "Mine is that our partner helped us with that. The same partner who has now taken away half my budget."

"Private companies are simply cheaper. That's not our fault."

"Ah, I feel as if I've gone back to the nineties," the Russian snorted. "Perhaps now you'll tell me about the invisible hand of the market?"

Uh-oh, Ayers thought, time to change the subject.

"I get the impression this conversation isn't heading in a constructive direction," he said with the cordial smile only an American can paste onto his face.

"Really?" Rybkin didn't let up. "Because as I see it, it's leading to some very specific conclusions."

"Zazhiganiye... Predvaritelnaya..." crackled over the speakers. The gantries swung back from the rocket, the launch cables fell away; it looked as if a flower were opening its petals. "Desyat! Devyat! Vosem!"

"Which ones, if I may ask?" asked the American.

"Sem! Shest! Pyat!"

"That all this" -- Rybkin pointed first at himself, then at Ayers -- "was a mistake."

Flame-light washed over their faces.

Just above Lucy's head dangled a little plush dog. It had a black nose, pink fur, and huge eyes. It weighed exactly twenty-three grams and was an important piece of soyuz equipment. It reminded her of a souvenir Nate had once brought back from a conference in Edinburgh. It was a stone in a frame labeled "Scottish Weather Station." If the stone is warm -- the sun is shining. If it's wet -- it's raining. If you can't see it -- it's foggy. The little dog's operating instructions would go like this. If the little dog doesn't move -- you haven't launched yet. If it starts to tremble -- the engines are on. If it starts bouncing -- you've lifted off. If it's floating in the air -- you've reached orbit. If it's on fire -- something has gone very wrong.

The little plush dog began to shake. So did her hands, legs, stomach, tongue, eyes, brain -- the vibrations spread through the whole capsule. Lucy sat -- or rather lay -- on the left-hand side, curled up with her knees pressed right up under her chin, wedged into a recess shaped from a plaster cast of her body; to get through a few hours in that position, everyone had preemptively swallowed a horse-sized dose of painkillers. In the cramped interior of the soyuz cabin, every cubic centimeter had to be used, so the storage lockers above the plexiglass bubble around her head bulged, packed to the brim. The capsule commander, Anton, was responsible for packing them. If the weight were distributed unevenly, if there were even half a kilogram more on one side than the other, the craft would veer off course. And when you were traveling through the vastness of space, that was never good news.

"Launch vehicle readings nominal," a voice came through her headset.

Lucy felt the immense force crushing her into the seat, squeezing the air from her lungs. Beneath her, forty thousand kilograms of rocket fuel burned. She glanced at the screen: the g-load was two g, two and a half, three, three and a half... She wanted to laugh, cry, shout with joy - at last, after eight years of waiting, work, self-denial, and sacrifices - her own and other people's - she was lifting off from Earth again. But she tamped it down, as efficiently and impassively as if she were throwing some lever on the control panel or pressing a button. For an astronaut, that skill mattered every bit as much as orbital mechanics or first aid. Lucy was always being watched, assessed, evaluated; even now, at this very moment, a camera pointed at her face, transmitting her image to mission control centers in Houston, Moscow, Toulouse, and Tsukuba. She knew that even though on this flight she was essentially a passenger (Anton and Lev were in control, and even they had little to do for the time being, because the onboard computer handled most of the tasks for them), she had to look serious and focused - otherwise she would end up on the radar of the psychologists responsible for the crew. So she followed the readouts on the screens and checked the next items on the checklist.

"First-stage separation," Anton said.

It felt as if someone had suddenly lifted their foot off the gas pedal. The rocket shed its tail, along with the empty tanks and four engines, each the size of a truck. Soon they would burn up in the atmosphere, like sacrifices offered up to gravity.

"Launch escape system separation."

A bang. The pointed tower housing the motors that, in an emergency, would have instantly torn the capsule away from the rest of the rocket snapped away. The most dangerous phase of the flight was behind them now. They could breathe again.

"Fairing separation."

Light flooded the capsule. Until then the windows had been covered by metal panels. Lucy looked out. Half the window was filled with a blackness without end, glittering with stars - seemingly the same ones she saw in the night sky, and yet different all the same: without the atmosphere's obscuring shroud, their light was much sharper, more distinct. She felt as though she were trying lenses at the optometrist's and had finally found the right pair, the ones that showed the world as it really was. Below, Earth slid past. Night lay over Asia, and city lights burned. She longed for this view. Every day. Every moment.

"Still impressive, huh?" Anton asked.

"It is."

"Second-stage separation," Lev read out the computer's message. Of the soaring tower that had lifted off from Baikonur, only the capsule remained now. "Third-stage ignition... initiated..."

"Just like that time in the Cupola," Anton went on. There was mockery in his voice. A challenge.

"Speed thirteen thousand seven hundred and two miles per hour," Lucy said, ignoring the provocation.

"We were over Turkmenistan then too."

"Solar panel deployment..." The astronaut flicked the appropriate switch. "Initiated..."

"Those lights there are Ashgabat... and over there, Bishkek..."

"Attention," came over the headsets. Flight controllers in Moscow. "Loss of signal in thirty seconds. Communications will resume in about twenty minutes. Begin preparations for the first delta-v maneuver."

"Copy."

Silence settled over the cabin. For exactly half a minute. The barbed words that followed never made it into the official record.

The Baikonur Cosmodrome began to empty out. The loudspeakers fell silent, the floodlights illuminating the launch pad went dark. Officials climbed into the limousines waiting for them, journalists folded up tripods and stowed cameras in their cases. Nate wondered what demand there would be for their material, who would want to air it and -- more importantly -- watch it. While the prospect of an expedition to the Moon or Mars still set imaginations alight, while people still got excited about the squabbles of billionaires zipping around in orbit, flights to the International Space Station had already become commonplace. News of one rocket launch after another was, of course, followed by a loyal circle of enthusiasts, but it no longer made the front pages of newspapers -- or even the second or third.

That was hardly surprising, really: the ISS was a laboratory after all -- a flying one, but still -- and how was anyone supposed to get excited about pipetting, even in weightlessness. NASA's press office understood that perfectly well. So it did what it could to drum up enthusiasm for the station: astronauts filmed videos explaining how the space toilet worked (look, these straps hold your feet in place, and you pee into this funnel...), spun around, squeezed juice from a tube, and caught droplets escaping from them, veritable circus performers of microgravity. But in the end even that lost its novelty. And so something that only half a century earlier had barely been a dream, really more of a fantasy, had already managed to work itself into everyday life, to slip soundlessly into the background. Such is the fate of every breakthrough, every technology: we are capable of getting used to anything. Perhaps that is the key to our success, the reason an animal made for life on a grassy savannah, a bipedal ape, has not yet gone mad in a world of glass, cables, and screens. Or perhaps rather: has not gone entirely mad.

"Dad, are we going?" Eliza asked. She was tired. A taxi was waiting downstairs.

"Just a minute."

Nate looked up at the sky, at the place where only a moment earlier the fire-breathing engines had flickered. There was no trace of the Soyuz anymore; it had dissolved among the stars, a dot among thousands of dots. The man took a deep breath -- he could smell exhaust fumes and hot asphalt -- then slowly let the air out of his lungs. The fear that had been devouring him for weeks, latching on to every thought and seeping into every dream, finally eased a little. But it did not disappear, because it never did: it was ready to return at the first opportunity, at the slightest trigger, like a spider that feels even the faintest tremor in the web strung inside his head. That was how he imagined it: thousands of silver threads stretched inside his skull, and among them gleam black eyes that never blink.

That was why Nate was glad to be going home, back to everyday life, to the familiar routes between home and school, the store and the gas station, the park and the library. He needed routine to tame reality, to cope with it. That, probably more than anything else, was what set him and Lucy apart. She was always hungry for new experiences; he shied away from them, even when the thing in question seemed pleasant enough, simple enough. A weekend away, say. Sounds great, but what if the weather doesn't cooperate, they run out of gas, get a flat, Eliza comes down sick, or they arrive to find something wrong with the reservation? Of course they went in the end — because he did want to go, after all, or at least knew he ought to want to — but the questions didn't disappear. So Nate worked out ever more elaborate contingency plans in his head, branching like the Amazon delta, and took wide-ranging preventive measures. He filled the tank the day before, checked the tire pressure and the oil, made doubly sure the rain jackets were packed and the hotel room booked for the right date. So Nate wasn't going on vacation so much as on something like three-person military maneuvers, during which, as chief of staff, he had to keep checking maps, taking reports, and identifying potential threats. Relaxing under those conditions was, obviously, difficult. He admired the scenery only after they got home, in photographs.

Lucy was his total opposite. Always composed, always in control. To her, a new problem was an interesting challenge, a puzzle to solve. In a rush to the airport and they hit traffic? Great — time to work out an alternate route. A piece was due in a few hours and the computer froze? A perfect opportunity to learn a little more about computing. Nothing could trip her up, nothing could throw her off.

They had met fourteen years earlier at the International Planetary Science Conference. He, a geophysicist, was then part of the team working on a probe that was supposed to go to Titan (it never did; they ran out of money). She, still an astrophysicist back then, was presenting the results of a spectroscopic analysis of the planets in the Gliese 581 system in Libra. He noticed her because, instead of a folding stand, her poster hung from a makeshift support made of a broom handle, a straightened wire hanger, and thread from the hotel's sewing kit. It turned out the airline had lost her luggage, so she had had to put together a new stand out of whatever she had on hand, and do it in under an hour. They talked for a while, he made a joke, she laughed. He asked if she had any plans for the evening. She didn't. Sometimes — rarely, but still — he regretted not asking what her plans were for the next ten years as well. Who knows, maybe then they wouldn't have gone for drinks, kissed in the hotel elevator, spent the night in his room — in short, that whole chain of cause and effect that had brought Nate here, to a cosmodrome in Kazakhstan, wouldn't have existed. Astronauts' partners had it hard. Partners with his kind of psychological make-up had it especially hard.

"Nate!" Someone laid a hand on his shoulder. Deputy Director Ayers. Though they had only met twice before, he sounded as if he were addressing his best friend. You could tell he had spent his whole life in politics. He had a reputation as an operator as skilled as he was ruthless. "Will you be at the farewell dinner?"

"Sorry, not this time. The little one needs to get to bed."

"I understand. If you need anything..."

"Thank you, we're all set."

"Excellent... Then we'll all be seeing each other here again in six months?"

If everything goes the way it should, said the voice in the back of Nate's head. If Lucy's capsule doesn't come in too steep and burn up. If it doesn't come in too shallow and skip off the sheath of gas around the planet like a flat stone skimmed across a lake. If the heat shield doesn't fail. If the cabin doesn't decompress. If the parachute opens. If they don't go into a spin that brings on lethal g-forces. If they don't smash into the Kazakh steppe.

"Exactly," he replied, struggling to force a smile.



The latches clanged as they were released and the hatch began to open. The Soyuz filled with the deafening roar of fans and the characteristic smells she remembered from her previous stay: sweat, heated metal, stale plastic. In the open hatchway above, the crew's faces appeared. Anton came out first -- one light push and he was already floating toward the exit, as if he were swimming through the air. Lucy swept aside the hair floating in front of her face and tied it back tight with an elastic band. She was ready; she could hardly wait. Now Lev -- one movement of his hand and he was up. It was her turn. She stood on the seat, bent her knees slightly, and flew after the rest.

Although she had been here before, although she had taken hundreds of parabolic flights, getting her body used to weightlessness, her head still spun and her stomach clenched. Up, down -- suddenly the words had lost their meaning, become useless. She felt as though she had found herself inside an optical illusion in which nothing was where it ought to be, one that tangled the senses more and more the longer you looked at it. Lucy tasted sour saliva at the back of her throat. Don't puke, she thought, just don't puke. Someone was already hugging her, slapping her on the back. She almost hit her head on a camera lens. They were in the Russian segment, in the Rassvet module, so it was cramped, too cramped for two people, let alone nine. They writhed like fish in a net.

Lucy recognized three of the astronauts they were relieving; their Soyuz was due to return to Earth in a few days: Igor Volkov, Kate Hortchak, Francesco Russo. A moment later the Dragon pilot Ezra Wendley greeted her. Red-haired, muscular, white as a sheet, he used to be in the military, and looked exactly like it. His capsule had docked three months earlier and was to remain here for another three. On her left she caught a glimpse of Lafayette Summers, the ever-smiling botanist, an African American from Louisiana. Last in line - though line was hardly the word for it, because everyone was in motion; it would have been easier for her to plan a checkmating move in three-dimensional chess than to say who was where at any given moment, whose hand this was and whose leg that - was Devaki Anand, an American of Indian descent. Lucy knew her the least. She alone was not a professional astronaut but a space tourist. That did not, of course, mean she could sip drinks from a glass with a little palm tree; NASA had pressed this crewmate into service too. How much she had paid for her stay on the station was a secret, but it was undoubtedly a seven-figure sum. For Devaki, who had made billions in Silicon Valley, that kind of expense was hardly a problem.

Lucy longed to get to her cabin already (a grand word - in reality it was a space smaller than a broom closet), get out of her clothes (though nobody said it aloud, astronauts flew in diapers, officially and euphemistically called "maximum absorbency garments"), and wash up (that is: wipe her whole body with disposable wet wipes, because there were no showers; free-floating water plus microgravity equals a guaranteed short circuit). But there was still one more item on the program: the press conference that capped the flight. They moved to Nauka, one of the station's oldest modules and one of its newest. Oldest, because construction on it had begun back in the nineties. Newest, because the Russians had only managed to finish it and send it into space at the beginning of this year. What they had not managed was a safe docking: when Nauka was attached, its previously malfunctioning main engine unexpectedly kicked in, knocking the entire station out of orbit, which very nearly ended in catastrophe. Inside, the module looked like any other: cables, lights, electronics jutted out everywhere, from every surface.

"Okay, let's get into place..." Volkov, the station crew commander for this rotation, took charge. "New ones up front... Rest in back..."

It took them a moment to get into their assigned positions. To avoid confusing viewers on Earth, they all adopted the same orientation, as though they were standing on a floor, though half of them might just as well have been hanging upside down. Everyone grabbed hold of something - those who had only just arrived at the station used their hands by reflex, while the rest, already at home in microgravity, hooked their feet under handrails; that was easier. Three, two, one: the light beside the camera's glass eye turned red.

"ISS, this is Moscow," the controller called. "Not too cramped in there?"

"We're managing somehow," Volkov replied with a smile. Like the rest of the Russians, he kept his hair cropped short, and his neck was red with irritation; shaving in space was particularly unpleasant. Above his head gleamed a gold Orthodox cross stuck to the wall.

"Excellent. Soyuz, how was the flight?"

"No trouble at all," Anton replied curtly.
